lesson.menu_title: Block 3 -- Measuring the Change & Reporting to the Board
lesson.menu_desc: Report progress and results to the board: what the team has been doing, how the metrics have moved, and what is on track -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Block 3 -- Measuring the Change & Reporting to the Board
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Vinicius Vale Bassan | Aula 13 -- Measuring the Change & Reporting to the Board
phases: How Long It Has Been Happening

listenings.text: Carlos, before the board, let me brief you on where the numbers stand. First, the headline: the integration is on track. We have been tracking five metrics against the baseline since day one, and four of them are now above target. Second, adoption. The teams have been using the new system for three months, and adoption has been climbing every week; we are now at eighty-five percent against a target of ninety. Third, engagement. We have been running a weekly pulse survey, and engagement has been rising steadily since the town hall, which tells us the people side is working. Fourth, the one we have to watch: headcount cost. We have not hit the savings target yet, because two teams have been waiting on the new tools, but the trend is good and we expect to close the gap by quarter end. So the message to the board is simple and honest: most of the change is ahead of plan, one metric is behind but improving, and we have the dashboard to prove it. Lead with the results, show the trend, and do not hide the one number that is lagging. Boards trust the leader who reports the gap before they ask about it.

Hi Carlos, this is Diana, with a quick recap after the board update today. Overall, the directors were impressed. You led with the results, you showed how the metrics have moved against the baseline, and you were honest about the one number that is behind, which built a lot of trust in the room. A few notes for next time. First, the dashboard: it is clear, but slow down on it; some directors needed a moment to read the trend, so walk them through it line by line. Second, the headcount metric: you handled the gap well, but the board wants a date, so tell them exactly when we expect to be on track. Third, adoption: that story landed perfectly; eighty-five against ninety, climbing every week, is exactly the kind of progress they want to hear. One thing to add next time: a short note on what we have learned, not just what we have measured, because the board values the lesson as much as the number. Send me the updated dashboard by Thursday, and we should review the lagging metric together before the next cycle. Strong update, Carlos. The board has confidence in this change, and so do I. Talk soon.
